title: Translation and Shearing
concept: Translation shifts an image horizontally or vertically, while shearing slants it along an axis. These geometric transforms simulate camera movements and perspective distortions, making your model far more robust.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main risk of using extremely small crops (e.g. 5% of the image area) as augmentation?

options.text: You might crop out the labeled object entirely, leaving only background
You will double the number of channels in the image
The model will automatically stop training due to formatting errors

exercises.explain: Yes! Aggressive crops risk losing the subject entirely.

cards.title: Distorted Perspectives
cards.body: When a user snaps a photo of a document or a barcode, they rarely hold the camera perfectly perpendicular. The camera is usually slightly tilted or offset, slanting the image. We need our CNN to handle these slight perspective skews.

cards.title: Translation and Shearing
cards.body: Translation shifts the image horizontally or vertically by a fraction of its width or height, filling empty edge pixels with zeros (black). Shearing slants the image along an axis, keeping one side fixed while sliding the opposite side parallel to it.

cards.title: Shaking a bowl of jelly
cards.body: Imagine a bowl of jelly with a cherry in the middle. Shifting the bowl left or right is like translation — the cherry moves, but stays upright. Nudging only the top of the jelly so it slants sideways is like shearing — the cherry tilts and deforms slightly. Both test if you can still spot the cherry!

cards.title: Optical Character Recognition (OCR)
cards.body: In document scanning (OCR), shearing is incredibly useful. It simulates italicized fonts or handwriting that slants forward, teaching the model that a slanted 'H' is still the letter 'H'.

cards.title: Not all shifts are clean cuts
cards.body: When you translate an image, you slide it out of its frame, leaving blank space behind. If you shift it too far, you might slide the target object completely out of bounds. Translation range is usually kept small (e.g., ±10% of the image size) to keep the target visible.

left.text:
Slides pixels along X or Y
Maintains upright shape
Leaves empty black borders

right.text:
Slants pixels along X or Y
Deforms right angles into slants
Simulates perspective tilts

exercises.prompt: Which transformation slants an image along an axis, distorting its parallel lines into sloped angles?
exercises.explain: Correct! Shearing slants the image, distorting its corners into sloped angles.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Translating an image by 20 pixels left leaves empty space on the right edge that is typically filled with black pixels.
exercises.explain: True. Shifting an image leaves empty borders, which are usually filled with zeros (black) or padded in other ways.

exercises.sentence: ___ shifts an image horizontally or vertically, while shearing slants it.
exercises.explain: Translation moves the entire image vertically or horizontally.

exercises.prompt: For which of these tasks is shearing especially helpful as an augmentation step?

options.text: Recognizing handwritten text where characters slant at various angles
Permanently removing noise from camera sensors
Converting color images to HSV space

exercises.explain: Correct! Shearing simulates natural handwriting slants, making it perfect for OCR text training.

exercises.prompt: A developer sets the translation range to 80% of the image size. Why is this a problem?

options.text: It will shift the main object completely out of the frame in most images, leaving the model to train on blank pixels
It will make the image files too large to fit in memory
It will automatically convert the image to grayscale
It will cause the convolutional layers to skip calculation

exercises.explain: Exactly! Massive shifts push objects completely out of frame. Next: how to balance all these transformations!
